title: Distinction between civilians and combatants
summary: The principle of distinction requires parties to an armed conflict to distinguish at all times between civilians and combatants, and between civilian objects and military objectives. Only combatants and military objectives may be lawfully targeted. The principle is universally recognised as the foundational norm of international humanitarian law, confirmed by the International Court of Justice in the Nuclear Weapons Advisory Opinion (1996) as a cardinal principle. State practice is reflected in the military manuals of virtually every state, including non-parties to Additional Protocol I such as the United States and Israel. Opinio juris is evidenced by consistent treaty practice, UN General Assembly resolutions, and statements by states asserting the customary status of the rule. Disputes arise in non-international armed conflicts regarding the definition of 'direct participation in hostilities' and the status of continuous combatants.

state_practice.description: US military manuals (Army FM 27-10, DoD Law of War Manual 2015) codify distinction as binding customary law applicable in all conflicts
UK Manual of the Law of Armed Conflict (2004) affirms distinction as a fundamental customary principle binding on all states
Israel Supreme Court (Targeted Killings case, 2006) applied the principle of distinction to assess legality of targeted killing operations

opinio_juris.description: ICJ Nuclear Weapons Advisory Opinion affirmed distinction as an intransgressible principle of customary international law
UNGA Resolution 2444 (1968) unanimously affirmed the principle of distinction as applicable in all armed conflicts

title: Prohibition of indiscriminate attacks
summary: Indiscriminate attacks — those not directed at a specific military objective, using methods that cannot be directed at a specific military objective, or causing effects that cannot be limited as required — are prohibited under customary international law. The rule derives from the principle of distinction and is confirmed in Additional Protocol I Article 51(4), which the ICRC considers reflective of custom binding all states. State practice, including military manuals of non-AP I states such as the United States, acknowledges the prohibition. Key instances include ICTY findings in the Martić case regarding cluster munitions used in civilian areas, and the UN Commission of Inquiry findings on aerial bombardments in Syria and Yemen. The main area of dispute concerns the definition of 'indiscriminate' in the context of area bombardment and the use of certain munitions including cluster bombs and white phosphorus in populated areas.

state_practice.description: US DoD Law of War Manual (2015) recognises prohibition on attacks that are indiscriminate by nature
ICTY, Prosecutor v Martić (2007): conviction for rocket attacks on Zagreb using cluster munitions as indiscriminate attacks

opinio_juris.description: ICTY Appeals Chamber in Galić (2006) confirmed the customary status of the prohibition on indiscriminate attacks including terror bombing
UN Security Council Resolution 1973 (2011) cited protection of civilians from indiscriminate attacks as basis for authorising force in Libya

title: Principle of proportionality in attack
summary: The proportionality principle prohibits attacks where the anticipated incidental civilian casualties or damage to civilian objects would be excessive in relation to the concrete and direct military advantage anticipated. Unlike distinction, proportionality involves a balancing judgment — it does not prohibit civilian casualties absolutely, but requires military commanders to refrain from attacks where harm is disproportionate to the military benefit. The ICRC considers ICRC Rule 14 as reflective of customary law binding all states. The principle was central to ICTY findings in the Galić case and has been applied in Israeli Supreme Court decisions on targeted killings and aerial operations. The concept of 'concrete and direct military advantage' and the standard for measuring anticipated civilian harm are the principal areas of legal and factual dispute, particularly in densely populated urban warfare.

state_practice.description: Israeli Supreme Court (Targeted Killings, 2006) applied proportionality as binding customary IHL in assessment of counter-terrorism operations
US DoD Law of War Manual (2015) chapter 5 codifies proportionality standard as customary and treaty-based obligation on all US forces

opinio_juris.description: ICTY in Galić (2006) confirmed proportionality as an established norm of customary IHL requiring military commanders to cancel or suspend attacks if civilian harm is excessive
UNGA Resolution 2675 (1970) affirmed protection of civilian populations and the principle that civilian harm must not be disproportionate to military objectives

title: Precautions in attack
summary: Precautions in attack require that those who plan or decide on attacks take all feasible precautions to avoid or minimise incidental civilian loss, including verifying that targets are military objectives, choosing means and methods that minimise civilian harm, and giving effective advance warning when civilian presence is expected. ICRC Rule 15 reflects the customary content of AP I Article 57. The obligation is one of due diligence — feasibility depends on available intelligence, operational tempo, and resources. The ICTY in the Kupreškić case (2000) held that the duty to take precautions is part of customary international law. UN investigations into conflicts in Gaza, Afghanistan, and Yemen have applied precautions analysis as customary law. Disputes centre on what intelligence threshold triggers a warning obligation and whether automated or AI-assisted targeting systems can satisfy the precautions requirement.

state_practice.description: NATO Coalition targeting procedures in Afghanistan (ISAF Tactical Directive 2009) implemented precautions requirements including pattern-of-life analysis, positive identification, and escalation of force
Israel's practice of roof-knocking and warning calls before strikes on buildings, upheld in Israeli courts as implementing customary precautions obligation

opinio_juris.description: ICTY in Kupreškić et al (2000) confirmed precautions as an established rule of customary IHL, stating the rule reflects military necessity balanced against protection of civilians
Universal adoption of AP I Art 57 by 174 states (including after 1977) and military manual acceptance by non-parties reflects opinio juris

title: Prohibition of torture and cruel treatment of detainees
summary: Customary IHL prohibits torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment of all persons deprived of liberty in armed conflict, whether international or non-international. ICRC Rule 90 reflects this norm, derived from Common Article 3 to the Geneva Conventions and Additional Protocol II. The prohibition applies to all detainees — prisoners of war, civilian internees, and persons detained in non-international armed conflicts. The ICTY confirmed in Delalić et al (1998) that the prohibition constitutes customary law in both types of conflict. US detention practices at Guantánamo Bay and Abu Ghraib generated intense scrutiny. The principal controversy concerns the definition of the threshold between torture and cruel treatment, and the status of so-called enhanced interrogation techniques under customary law.
primary_codification: ICRC Rule 90; Geneva Convention III Art. 17

state_practice.description: US Army Field Manual 2-22.3 (2006) on interrogation codifies prohibition on torture and cruel treatment as binding customary and treaty law applicable to all detainees
ICRC confidential reports on Guantánamo (2004) asserted customary IHL prohibition applies to all persons in US custody regardless of legal status

opinio_juris.description: ICTY in Delalić et al (Čelebići, 1998) confirmed customary status of the prohibition on torture in non-international armed conflicts under Common Article 3
Universal ratification of Geneva Conventions (196 states) and Common Article 3 confirms opinio juris on the prohibition as applicable in all armed conflicts

title: Prohibition of attacks on protected objects (hospitals, cultural property)
summary: Customary IHL prohibits directing attacks against protected objects including civilian hospitals, medical units and transport, places of worship, and cultural property. These protections derive from the 1954 Hague Convention on Cultural Property, Additional Protocol I Articles 53–56, and ICRC Customary Rules 28–30 and 38–42. Protections are not absolute — they are lost if objects are used for hostile purposes — but require a warning before protection is withdrawn. The ICTY confirmed in Strugar (2005) that attacks on protected cultural property constitute war crimes under customary IHL. The destruction of cultural property in Timbuktu led to the first ICC prosecution solely for cultural property destruction (Al Mahdi, 2016), affirming the norm's application in non-international armed conflicts.

state_practice.description: Universal participation in the 1954 Hague Convention (133 states) and near-universal AP I ratification (174 states) evidence consistent state protection of cultural property and hospitals in armed conflict
NATO coalition forces in Iraq (2003) maintained no-strike lists for hospitals and cultural sites, implemented as customary IHL obligations in targeting procedures

opinio_juris.description: ICC Al Mahdi judgment (2016) confirmed intentional attacks on religious and historic buildings constitute war crimes under customary international law in non-international armed conflicts
ICTY in Strugar (2005) convicted defendant for attacks on the Old Town of Dubrovnik as a war crime under customary IHL, confirming the customary status of protections for cultural property

title: Humane treatment of prisoners of war
summary: The customary norm requiring humane treatment of prisoners of war (POWs) prohibits murder, torture, cruel treatment, outrages upon personal dignity, and requires adequate food, shelter, and medical care for captured combatants. The Third Geneva Convention (1949) is the principal treaty instrument, but the core protections are universally accepted as reflecting customary international law binding all parties to armed conflict. The ICJ in the Nuclear Weapons Advisory Opinion (1996) confirmed that the Geneva Conventions' core principles are intransgressible norms of customary IHL. Post-9/11, the US government's designation of Taliban and Al-Qaeda detainees as unlawful combatants ineligible for POW status generated major controversy about the scope and limits of customary humane treatment obligations.

state_practice.description: US Supreme Court in Hamdan v Rumsfeld (2006) held Common Article 3 applies to Guantánamo detainees as a matter of customary law, regardless of POW status determinations
ICRC delegates' access to POWs in all major 20th and 21st century conflicts reflects uniform state practice of accepting GC III as a customary obligation

opinio_juris.description: ICJ Nuclear Weapons Advisory Opinion (1996) held Geneva Convention core protections including humane treatment of POWs constitute intransgressible principles of customary IHL
Universal ratification of GC III (196 states) demonstrates global opinio juris that humane treatment of POWs is a legally obligatory norm binding all states

title: Prohibition of torture as a peremptory norm (jus cogens)
summary: The prohibition of torture constitutes a peremptory norm of general international law (jus cogens) from which no derogation is permitted. It applies to state agents in all circumstances, whether in peacetime, armed conflict, or public emergency. The norm encompasses both physical and mental suffering intentionally inflicted for purposes including obtaining information, punishment, intimidation, or discrimination. The absolute nature of the prohibition distinguishes it from most human rights norms that permit emergency derogation. The House of Lords in A v Secretary of State (2005) and the European Court of Human Rights in Ireland v UK (1978) and subsequent cases confirmed the non-derogable character. The ICJ in Questions Relating to the Obligation to Prosecute (2012) treated the prohibition as a peremptory norm with erga omnes effect. Debate persists on the boundary between torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment, particularly regarding 'enhanced interrogation' techniques.
primary_codification: CAT Art. 1; ICJ, Questions relating to the Obligation to Prosecute (2012)

state_practice.description: Universal ratification of the UN Convention Against Torture (CAT) — 173 state parties — reflects global acceptance of the prohibition as legally binding
UK House of Lords in A v Secretary of State for the Home Department (2005) held evidence obtained by torture inadmissible as a matter of common law and international law

opinio_juris.description: ICJ confirmed torture prohibition as peremptory norm (jus cogens) with erga omnes effect in Questions Relating to Obligation to Prosecute (2012)
ECtHR, Ireland v United Kingdom (1978) held prohibition on torture under Article 3 ECHR to be absolute, admitting no exceptions

title: Prohibition of enforced disappearance
summary: The prohibition of enforced disappearance — the arrest, detention or abduction of persons by agents of the state, followed by a refusal to acknowledge the deprivation of liberty or reveal the fate or whereabouts of the missing person — constitutes a serious violation of international human rights law. The Inter-American Court of Human Rights established the norm in Velásquez Rodríguez v Honduras (1988), the first major international judgment on disappearances. The norm combines violations of the right to liberty, the right not to be subjected to torture, and the right to recognition as a person before the law. The UN Declaration on Enforced Disappearance (1992) and the International Convention for the Protection of All Persons from Enforced Disappearance (ICPPED, 2006) codify the prohibition. The customary status is broadly accepted, though the threshold between isolated acts and systematic practices affecting the norm's characterisation as a crime against humanity remains debated.

state_practice.description: Universal condemnation in UNGA and Human Rights Council resolutions of enforced disappearances as a grave violation of international law across all regional groups
Argentina's prosecutions of military officers for enforced disappearances during 1976–83 as crimes under international law — a landmark exercise of domestic enforcement of the customary norm

opinio_juris.description: IACtHR in Velásquez Rodríguez (1988) held Honduras responsible under customary international law for systematic enforced disappearances, establishing state duty to investigate and provide reparations
UNGA Declaration on the Protection of All Persons from Enforced Disappearance (Res 47/133, 1992) adopted by consensus — treated as authoritative statement of opinio juris

title: Prohibition of summary execution
summary: The customary prohibition of summary execution forbids deliberate killings by state agents outside any lawful process — without judicial proceedings, trial, or under a manifestly unlawful order. It extends to extrajudicial killings, death squad activity attributable to the state, and targeted killings without legal basis. The UN Principles on Extra-Legal Executions (1989) set authoritative standards. The norm is reinforced by Article 6 ICCPR (right to life) as customary law and confirmed by the Human Rights Committee in General Comment 36 (2018). The IACtHR and ECtHR have developed extensive jurisprudence affirming state responsibility. The principal dispute concerns targeted killing of alleged terrorists — whether this constitutes summary execution or lawful use of force — and what procedural safeguards are required before lethal force may be used.
primary_codification: ICCPR Art. 6; UN Principles on Extra-legal Executions (1989)

state_practice.description: El Salvador's Truth Commission (1993) documented and condemned state-sponsored death squad activity during civil war as violations of the customary prohibition on summary execution
Philippines documented thousands of extrajudicial killings in anti-drug campaign (2016–) — subject to ICC preliminary examination as violations of the customary prohibition

opinio_juris.description: UN Human Rights Committee General Comment No 36 on Article 6 ICCPR (2018) confirms prohibition on arbitrary deprivation of life as a norm of customary international law
IACtHR in Neira Alegría v Peru (1995) held state responsible for summary execution of prisoners under customary law duty to protect right to life

title: Non-refoulement obligation
summary: Non-refoulement prohibits states from returning any person to a territory where they would face a real risk of persecution, torture, or other serious harm. Enshrined in Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and Article 3 of the UN Convention Against Torture, the principle is widely regarded as having attained customary international law status. UNHCR and most scholars maintain the customary norm applies to all persons regardless of their refugee status. The ECtHR in Soering v UK (1989) held that extradition to face treatment contrary to Article 3 ECHR violates the Convention. The norm is contested in relation to interception at sea (where states deny arrival in their territory), mass influx situations, and the scope of the national security exception. The principle's absolute character vis-à-vis the security exception remains the most live dispute.

state_practice.description: Near-universal state practice of non-refoulement in domestic asylum systems — embodied in national refugee laws of over 140 states incorporating the 1951 Convention standard
ECtHR found Italy violated non-refoulement in Hirsi Jamaa v Italy (2012) by intercepting Somali and Eritrean migrants at sea and returning them to Libya without individual assessment

opinio_juris.description: ECtHR in Soering v UK (1989) established that Article 3 ECHR contains an implicit non-refoulement obligation — applied by courts of 47 states as reflecting customary law
UNHCR Executive Committee Conclusions Nos 6, 17, 25 affirm non-refoulement as a cardinal principle accepted by the international community with customary status

title: Right to a fair trial as a customary norm
summary: Whether the right to a fair trial has attained customary international law status beyond its treaty basis in ICCPR Article 14 and regional conventions is genuinely contested. Core elements — the right to be heard, equality of arms, an independent and impartial tribunal, and the right to be informed of charges — are widely accepted. The Human Rights Committee affirms ICCPR Article 14 as universal minimum standards. However, scholars and courts disagree on which specific procedural guarantees form part of custom, as opposed to treaty obligations binding only state parties. The ICTY and ICTR applied many fair trial guarantees as custom in their proceedings. The main disputed questions are whether the right applies in non-international armed conflicts and whether it constrains military commissions for alien unlawful combatants.

state_practice.description: US Military Commissions Act (2006) controversially limited certain fair trial rights for Guantánamo detainees, prompting wide international criticism as a violation of customary fair trial standards
The African Court on Human and Peoples' Rights has applied fair trial guarantees as customary norms in multiple cases against states not party to relevant specific treaties

opinio_juris.description: UN Human Rights Committee General Comment No 32 (2007) treats core fair trial guarantees under ICCPR Art 14 as minimum standards reflecting customary international law
UNSC unanimously adopted ICTY and ICTR Statutes codifying fair trial guarantees — applied as customary law in international criminal proceedings

title: Prohibition of racial discrimination
summary: The prohibition of racial discrimination constitutes a peremptory norm of customary international law (jus cogens). The ICJ in Barcelona Traction (1970) identified obligations erga omnes which include prohibitions on racial discrimination, giving all states a legal interest in their observance. The International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination (CERD, 1965) has 182 state parties and is widely treated as reflective of customary obligations. The norm encompasses both direct discrimination and measures with disproportionate discriminatory effect. The apartheid regime in South Africa was treated as a paradigm violation of customary prohibitions on racial discrimination, contributing to the hardening of the norm. The ILC's 2022 Conclusions on Jus Cogens include prohibition of racial discrimination in the illustrative list.
primary_codification: CERD; ICJ, Barcelona Traction (1970) — erga omnes obligations

state_practice.description: Universal condemnation of South African apartheid through UNGA and UNSC resolutions and a sanctions regime — treated as violation of customary norm against racial discrimination
Near-universal ratification of CERD (182 states) and incorporation into domestic legal systems reflects consistent state practice treating racial discrimination prohibition as binding customary law

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Barcelona Traction (1970) identified obligations erga omnes arising from customary international law, including prohibition of racial discrimination — all states have a legal interest in compliance
ILC Conclusions on Jus Cogens (2022) include prohibition of racial discrimination and apartheid as a peremptory norm in the illustrative list

title: Obligation to make full reparation for internationally wrongful acts
summary: The obligation to make full reparation for internationally wrongful acts is a foundational norm of customary international law, articulated by the Permanent Court of International Justice in the Chorzów Factory case (1928) as the principle that a state which has committed an internationally wrongful act must wipe out all the consequences of the illegal act and re-establish the situation which would have existed if that act had not been committed. Reparation may take three forms: restitution (restoring the prior situation), compensation (payment for financially assessable damage), and satisfaction (acknowledgment, apology, or assurances of non-repetition). The ILC Articles on State Responsibility (2001) codify these forms in Articles 34–37 and are widely regarded as reflecting customary international law. The norm applies between states but its extension to reparation owed directly to individuals remains contested.
primary_codification: ILC ARSIWA Art. 31; PCIJ, Chorzów Factory (1928)

state_practice.description: Iran-US Claims Tribunal (1981–present) applied Chorzów Factory standard as customary law in hundreds of compensation awards
Germany paid reparations to Israel under Luxembourg Agreement (1952) acknowledging state obligation arising from wrongful acts

opinio_juris.description: PCIJ Chorzów Factory case established that reparation is the indispensable complement to the failure to apply a convention — confirmed as customary by subsequent international courts
ILC Articles on State Responsibility Art 31, adopted by UNGA without vote in 2001, treated as authoritative restatement of customary law by ICJ and international tribunals

title: Attribution of conduct to states
summary: Attribution determines when conduct of individuals or entities is legally treated as acts of the state, triggering state responsibility. The ILC ARSIWA codify the primary rules: conduct of state organs (Article 4), persons or entities exercising governmental authority (Article 5), organs placed at the disposal of another state (Article 6), conduct directed or controlled by the state (Article 8), and conduct adopted by the state (Article 11). The Nicaragua effective control test and the ICTY Tadić overall control test represent competing standards for attribution of non-state actor conduct. The ICJ in Bosnia v Serbia (2007) confirmed the effective control standard for genocide attribution. Attribution of cyber operations and AI-system conduct are emerging frontiers. The divergence between ICJ and ICTY attribution standards for non-state actors remains the central doctrinal dispute.

state_practice.description: Iran held responsible by Iran-US Claims Tribunal for conduct of revolutionary organs and entities that became state organs after 1979 revolution
ICJ found Uganda responsible for acts of its armed forces (UPDF) in DRC, applying Article 4 ARSIWA as customary law on attribution of state organ conduct

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) established effective control test for attribution of non-state actor conduct as the applicable customary standard
UNGA adoption of ARSIWA without vote (Res 56/83, 2001) and subsequent widespread citation by international courts confirms customary status of attribution rules

title: Circumstances precluding wrongfulness (necessity, force majeure)
summary: Circumstances precluding wrongfulness are situations in which the otherwise wrongful character of a state act is excluded — not the primary obligation itself, but the secondary obligation of responsibility. The ILC ARSIWA Articles 20–27 codify six such circumstances: consent, self-defence, countermeasures, force majeure, distress, and necessity. Necessity requires that the act be the only means of safeguarding an essential interest against a grave and imminent peril, not seriously impairing an essential interest of another state or the international community. The ICJ in Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros (1997) confirmed the customary status of necessity but stressed its exceptional and stringent character. The Argentina investment treaty arbitrations of the early 2000s generated controversy about whether economic crisis could constitute necessity, producing inconsistent arbitral decisions that highlighted the contested content of the doctrine.

state_practice.description: Argentina invoked necessity before ICSID tribunals following 2001 economic crisis — tribunals applied ARSIWA Article 25 as customary law with inconsistent results, some accepting the defence
Hungary invoked ecological necessity before the ICJ in Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros to justify suspension of treaty obligations — the ICJ confirmed the doctrine but rejected the claim on the facts

UNGA adoption of ARSIWA (Res 56/83, 2001) without vote reflects broad acceptance that circumstances precluding wrongfulness reflect customary law

title: Customary right to take countermeasures
summary: Countermeasures are otherwise wrongful acts taken by an injured state in response to an internationally wrongful act of another state, intended to induce compliance with international obligations. The ILC ARSIWA Articles 49–54 codify the customary rules: countermeasures must be directed at the responsible state, must be proportionate, must be preceded by a call for cessation and offer of negotiations, and must not involve peremptory norm violations or affect diplomatic relations or third party rights. The ICJ in Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros (1997) confirmed the customary basis of the countermeasures doctrine. Ongoing disputes concern whether third states may take countermeasures for erga omnes violations (collective countermeasures), the permissibility of economic sanctions, and whether cyber operations may constitute proportionate countermeasures.

state_practice.description: Western states' sanctions against Russia following invasion of Ukraine (2022) characterised by some as collective countermeasures for erga omnes violations — raising questions of third-state standing under customary law

title: Erga omnes obligations and standing of third states
summary: Erga omnes obligations are owed by states to the international community as a whole, as distinct from ordinary bilateral obligations. The ICJ articulated the concept in Barcelona Traction (1970), identifying prohibitions on aggression, genocide, and racial discrimination as examples. The consequence is that all states have a legal interest in their protection and may invoke responsibility even without being individually injured. ARSIWA Article 48 codifies the right of non-injured states to invoke responsibility for erga omnes violations. The central disputes are: whether the concept has hardened sufficiently in customary law to give non-injured states enforceable standing before international courts; whether collective countermeasures for erga omnes breaches are lawful; and the precise list of norms with erga omnes character. The Gambia v Myanmar (2022) ICJ ruling significantly advanced the doctrine.
primary_codification: ICJ, Barcelona Traction (1970); ILC ARSIWA Art. 48

state_practice.description: Gambia brought genocide claim against Myanmar (2019) as a non-injured state on the erga omnes character of Genocide Convention obligations — ICJ accepted standing in 2022 preliminary objections judgment
Canada and Netherlands initiated inter-state complaint against Syria before the Committee Against Torture (2021) as non-directly injured states, invoking erga omnes character of CAT obligations

title: Prohibition of recognising unlawful situations (non-recognition)
summary: The principle of non-recognition requires states not to recognise as lawful situations created through serious violations of peremptory norms of international law — including territorial acquisition by force — and not to render aid or assistance in maintaining such situations. ARSIWA Article 41(2) codifies the obligation as a consequence of serious breaches of jus cogens. The ICJ in the Namibia Advisory Opinion (1971) held states were under an obligation not to recognise the continued illegal presence of South Africa in Namibia. The norm has been applied to Israel's occupation, Russia's annexation of Crimea (2014) and additional Ukrainian territories (2022), and Northern Cyprus. The extent to which non-recognition is a positive legal obligation under customary law, rather than a political commitment, and its practical consequences remain debated.
primary_codification: ILC ARSIWA Art. 41; ICJ, Namibia Advisory Opinion (1971)

state_practice.description: Universal non-recognition of Russia's annexation of Crimea (2014) — UNGA Resolution 68/262 adopted by 100 states affirming non-recognition of the referendum and annexation as required by customary law

title: Inviolability of diplomatic premises
summary: The inviolability of diplomatic premises is a cornerstone of the law of diplomatic relations, prohibiting the receiving state from entering the premises of a diplomatic mission without consent, and imposing a duty to protect mission premises from intrusion, damage, or disturbance. The norm is codified in Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations (VCDR) Article 22 and confirmed as reflecting customary international law by the ICJ in the Tehran Hostages case (1980). The ICJ held that the obligation to protect premises is absolute and not conditional on the diplomatic relations between the states concerned. The rule applies regardless of the severity of the acts alleged against the sending state or its diplomats. State practice strongly and consistently supports inviolability. Controversies include whether the right of asylum in diplomatic premises has a customary basis, and the limits of the duty where states face security emergencies.

state_practice.description: Universal state practice of non-entry into foreign diplomatic premises — documented in VCDR travaux préparatoires and consistent post-1961 practice of 193 UN member states
UK refusal to enter Ecuadorian Embassy premises to arrest Julian Assange (2012–2019) despite pressure, citing VCDR inviolability obligations

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Tehran Hostages confirmed duty of absolute inviolability of diplomatic premises as customary international law, stressing the obligation is unconditional
Near-universal ratification of the VCDR (193 state parties) demonstrates opinio juris that inviolability is legally obligatory

title: Personal inviolability of diplomatic agents
summary: The personal inviolability of diplomatic agents prohibits arrest or detention of diplomats in the receiving state under any circumstances, and imposes a duty to treat them with due respect and to protect them from any attack on their person, freedom, or dignity. VCDR Article 29 is universally treated as reflecting customary international law. The obligation is absolute and admits no exception — even where a diplomat is caught committing a serious crime. The receiving state's only remedy is to declare the diplomat persona non grata. The Tehran Hostages case reaffirmed this absolute character. The UK's handling of the Sacoolas case (2019) following the fatal road accident involving a US diplomat's wife generated significant public controversy about diplomatic immunity and accountability, illustrating ongoing tensions in the customary norm.

state_practice.description: Universal state practice of releasing and declaring persona non grata rather than arresting diplomatic agents suspected of criminal offences — documented consistently across all UN member states
UK declared US diplomat's wife Anne Sacoolas persona non grata after fatal road accident (2019) rather than prosecuting — asserting VCDR Art 29 personal inviolability as customary law

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Tehran Hostages (1980) confirmed that the personal inviolability of diplomatic agents constitutes one of the fundamental premises upon which diplomatic relations rest under customary international law

title: Diplomatic immunity from criminal jurisdiction
summary: Diplomatic agents enjoy absolute immunity from the criminal jurisdiction of the receiving state under VCDR Article 31. This immunity cannot be prosecuted in the receiving state for any criminal offence, however serious. The only remedies available to the receiving state are persona non grata declaration or waiver of immunity by the sending state under VCDR Article 32. The sending state retains jurisdiction and the diplomat may be prosecuted at home — immunity does not equate to impunity. The ICJ and ECtHR have confirmed this principle. Contemporary debates focus on serious crimes committed by diplomatic agents — sexual offences, manslaughter — and whether customary law is developing toward any limits on immunity in such cases. The Sacoolas case and similar incidents have intensified public and political pressure for reform.

state_practice.description: Consistent practice of all receiving states declining to prosecute diplomatic agents for criminal offences including serious crimes, and instead seeking waiver or declaring persona non grata
UK courts confirmed absolute immunity of diplomatic agent from criminal prosecution regardless of gravity of offence under VCDR Art 31 as customary law in multiple cases

opinio_juris.description: Universal VCDR ratification and consistent domestic court decisions affirming absolute criminal immunity reflect global opinio juris that VCDR Art 31 represents binding customary law
ECtHR confirmed that criminal immunity of diplomatic agents does not violate the right of access to court under ECHR Art 6 — treating immunity as a legitimate customary law constraint

title: Freedom of communication for diplomatic missions
summary: The freedom of communication norm under VCDR Article 27 guarantees diplomatic missions the right to communicate freely with their sending state and other missions, including through a diplomatic bag and courier that the receiving state may not open or detain. The inviolability of diplomatic communications is universally treated as customary international law. It encompasses all forms of official communication — written messages, electronic communications, and physical materials. The norm is subject to abuse — states have used diplomatic bags to transport prohibited goods — but the receiving state's remedy is limited to refusing the bag or declaring a courier persona non grata, not opening the bag. Contemporary controversies include electronic surveillance of diplomatic communications, particularly following the 2013 Snowden revelations of US surveillance of allied missions.

state_practice.description: Universal acceptance of diplomatic bag inviolability — no state claims a right to open diplomatic pouches, and receiving states consistently decline to search them even when suspicious of prohibited contents
US NSA surveillance of EU and German diplomatic missions (2013 Snowden revelations) triggered protests characterising surveillance as violation of VCDR Art 27 as customary law

opinio_juris.description: Universal VCDR ratification and ILC commentary confirms freedom of communication for missions is a foundational customary norm underpinning diplomatic relations

title: Consular access to detained nationals
summary: Article 36 of the Vienna Convention on Consular Relations (VCCR) requires that when a national of a sending state is arrested or detained, that person must be informed without delay of their right to have their consulate notified, and if they request it, the consular authorities must be informed. The ICJ in LaGrand (2001) and Avena (2004) held that Article 36 confers individual rights on detained foreign nationals, not merely interstate obligations. The norm is broadly treated as reflecting customary international law. The US Supreme Court in Medellín v Texas (2008) complicated enforcement by refusing to give direct effect to ICJ judgments ordering review of convictions. Disputes centre on the 'without delay' requirement, the obligation to inform detainees of their rights, and remedies for breach — particularly in capital cases.

state_practice.description: US Department of Homeland Security detention standards require notification of consular rights to foreign national detainees as a matter of domestic policy and VCCR obligation
Germany brought LaGrand case before ICJ after US executed German nationals without notifying German consulate, asserting VCCR Art 36 as customary law

title: Freedom of navigation on the high seas
summary: Freedom of navigation on the high seas grants all states, whether coastal or landlocked, the right to sail ships flying their flag on the high seas. This freedom encompasses navigation, overflight, laying cables and pipelines, construction of artificial islands, fishing, and scientific research. Codified in UNCLOS Article 87, the norm reflects centuries of customary practice and is confirmed as a rule of customary international law applicable to all states including non-UNCLOS parties. The United States, not a party to UNCLOS, consistently asserts freedom of navigation as customary law and conducts Freedom of Navigation Operations (FONOPs) to challenge excessive maritime claims. The major area of contemporary dispute concerns the extent to which high seas freedoms extend within the EEZ of another state, and the limits imposed by emerging norms of marine environment protection.

state_practice.description: United States Freedom of Navigation Program (FONOPS) — conducted since 1979 — challenges excessive maritime claims by over 80 states, asserting freedom of navigation as customary law
Universal state practice of flag state jurisdiction on high seas vessels — documented in national maritime codes and bilateral treaties of all major maritime powers

title: Right of innocent passage in the territorial sea
summary: The right of innocent passage entitles ships of all states to pass continuously and expeditiously through the territorial sea of coastal states without entering internal waters. Codified in UNCLOS Articles 17–32, the right is universally recognised as a customary rule confirmed by the ICJ in the Corfu Channel case (1949). The passage must be innocent — not prejudicial to the peace, good order, or security of the coastal state. Coastal states may regulate but not hamper innocent passage. Submarines must navigate on the surface. Warship innocent passage is the most contested issue: UNCLOS does not require prior consent, but several states including China, India, and Brazil claim a prior notification or consent requirement — a position the United States and other major naval powers challenge as contrary to customary law through Freedom of Navigation Operations.

state_practice.description: US Freedom of Navigation Operations challenge prior consent requirements for warship innocent passage asserted by China, India, and others as contrary to customary international law
USSR and United States issued 1989 Joint Statement affirming the right of warships to conduct innocent passage without prior notification — significant customary law statement

title: Exclusive economic zone sovereign rights
summary: The exclusive economic zone (EEZ) grants coastal states sovereign rights for exploring, exploiting, conserving, and managing natural resources in a maritime zone extending up to 200 nautical miles from baselines. UNCLOS Articles 55–75 codify the regime introduced in the 1970s. The EEZ concept rapidly attained customary international law status even before UNCLOS entered into force, as reflected in widespread state legislation confirmed in the ICJ's Libya/Malta case (1985). Within the EEZ, other states retain high seas freedoms of navigation, overflight, and laying cables. Key ongoing disputes concern the scope of coastal state jurisdiction over military activities and marine scientific research in the EEZ; excessive baseline claims that inflate EEZ entitlements; and China's expansive nine-dash line claim, which the South China Sea Arbitration (2016) found incompatible with UNCLOS.

state_practice.description: Virtually all coastal states have enacted EEZ legislation and claimed 200nm zones — over 130 EEZ claims documented since the 1970s, establishing rapid custom formation
China claims right to regulate foreign military activities in its EEZ — challenged by US FONOPs and inconsistent with the customary rule that high seas freedoms apply within EEZs

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Libya/Malta Continental Shelf case (1985) affirmed that the EEZ concept had rapidly become part of customary international law through state practice and opinio juris prior to UNCLOS entering into force

title: Continental shelf entitlement beyond 200 nautical miles
summary: The continental shelf entitles coastal states to sovereign rights over the seabed and subsoil for purposes of exploration and exploitation of natural resources, extending to at least 200 nautical miles and potentially further based on a geological formula. The ICJ's landmark North Sea Continental Shelf judgment (1969) established that continental shelf entitlement arises ipso facto by virtue of sovereignty over the land territory — it need not be proclaimed. The Court also identified equidistance as not a customary rule for delimitation, preferring equitable principles. UNCLOS Article 76 provides the detailed formula for extended continental shelf claims beyond 200nm. Over 80 states have submitted claims to the Commission on the Limits of the Continental Shelf. Delimitation between overlapping claims remains the principal source of litigation before the ICJ and ITLOS.
primary_codification: UNCLOS Art. 76; ICJ, North Sea Continental Shelf (1969)

state_practice.description: Over 80 state submissions to the CLCS for extended continental shelf claims beyond 200nm — reflects universal acceptance of the customary entitlement codified in UNCLOS Art 76
Bilateral delimitation agreements between Germany, Netherlands, and Denmark following ICJ North Sea judgment (1971–72) applied equitable principles as customary law

title: Prohibition of piracy and universal jurisdiction over pirates
summary: Piracy — illegal acts of violence, detention, or depredation committed for private ends by the crew of a private ship on the high seas against another ship — is subject to universal jurisdiction under customary international law. All states may seize pirate ships, arrest pirates, and prosecute them regardless of the nationality of the pirates or the location of the act. UNCLOS Articles 100–107 codify the customary rules. The duty to cooperate in the repression of piracy (Article 100) is a positive obligation binding all states. The Somali piracy crisis (2008–2012) generated extensive state practice and multiple UNSC resolutions confirming the customary basis of universal jurisdiction. Debates concern the definition of piracy (the private ends requirement excludes politically motivated acts), the status of armed robbery within territorial waters, and the adequacy of existing frameworks for contemporary maritime security.

state_practice.description: Multi-national naval operations (CTF-151, EU NAVFOR Atalanta, NATO Ocean Shield) against Somali piracy (2008–2012) conducted under assertion of customary universal jurisdiction
Kenyan, Seychellois, and Mauritian courts prosecuted Somali pirates under universal jurisdiction agreements endorsed by the UNSC — confirming the customary norm in practice

title: Duty to render assistance to persons in distress at sea
summary: The duty to render assistance requires every ship's master to render assistance to any person found at sea in danger of being lost, insofar as this can be done without serious danger to the ship, crew, or passengers. UNCLOS Article 98 codifies this customary rule, reinforced by the SAR Convention (1979) and SOLAS (1974). The duty is well established as customary international law. Contemporary controversy focuses on search and rescue obligations in migration and refugee contexts: whether states conducting push-back operations or closing ports to rescued migrants violate the customary duty, and whether the duty extends to coordinating rescue operations in another state's SAR zone. The ECtHR's ruling in Hirsi Jamaa v Italy (2012) addressed related state responsibility issues in push-back operations.

state_practice.description: Italy's closure of ports to NGO rescue vessels (2019–2023) and closed ports policy challenged by maritime law experts as violating the customary duty to allow disembarkation of rescued persons
Universal state acceptance of SAR coordination zones under the SAR Convention framework — 162 state parties treating rescue duty as binding customary law

title: No-harm principle (sic utere tuo)
summary: The no-harm principle, expressed in the maxim sic utere tuo ut alienum non laedas, prohibits states from allowing or conducting activities within their jurisdiction or control that cause significant transboundary harm to the environment of other states or areas beyond national jurisdiction. Recognised as a norm of customary international law by the ICJ in the Corfu Channel case (1949) and confirmed in the Nuclear Weapons Advisory Opinion (1996), the rule is also reflected in Stockholm Principle 21 and Rio Principle 2. The Trail Smelter arbitration (1941) remains the foundational precedent, establishing state responsibility for transboundary pollution. Contemporary application covers atmospheric pollution, river contamination, ocean dumping, and activities affecting the global commons. Disputes focus on the threshold of harm required to trigger the obligation and the relationship between the no-harm rule and the precautionary principle.
primary_codification: Trail Smelter Arbitration (1941); Stockholm Principle 21; Rio Principle 2

state_practice.description: Trail Smelter Arbitration (Canada/US, 1941): Canada acknowledged and paid compensation for transboundary sulphur dioxide pollution from Consolidated Mining smelter affecting Washington State

title: Obligation to conduct environmental impact assessments for transboundary harm
summary: The obligation to conduct environmental impact assessments (EIA) for activities likely to cause significant transboundary harm has been recognised as a rule of customary international law by the ICJ in the Pulp Mills case (2010). The Court held that the duty to conduct an EIA before undertaking potentially harmful activities is part of general international law where there is a risk of significant transboundary damage. ITLOS Seabed Mining Advisory Opinion (2011) confirmed EIA obligations in the deep seabed context. The obligation is procedural — it requires assessment before authorisation, not a particular outcome. Disputes concern the threshold of risk triggering the EIA obligation, the required content and rigor of the assessment, and whether the obligation extends to activities affecting areas beyond national jurisdiction such as the high seas and Antarctica.
primary_codification: ICJ, Pulp Mills (2010); ITLOS, Seabed Mining Advisory Opinion (2011)

state_practice.description: Over 100 states have enacted domestic EIA legislation applying to activities with potential transboundary effects — the Espoo Convention (1991) with 44 parties codifies the norm for European states
Uruguay failed to conduct adequate EIA before authorising pulp mills on the River Uruguay — ICJ found this violated customary international law in Pulp Mills (2010)

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Pulp Mills (2010) held that the obligation to conduct an EIA where there is risk of significant transboundary harm forms part of general international law
ITLOS Seabed Mining Advisory Opinion (2011) affirmed EIA as a customary obligation applicable to activities in the Area under the sponsoring state due diligence standard

title: Precautionary principle in environmental law
summary: The precautionary principle holds that where there are threats of serious or irreversible environmental damage, lack of full scientific certainty shall not be used as a reason for postponing cost-effective measures to prevent environmental degradation. Rio Declaration Principle 15 is the standard formulation. The principle's status as customary international law is contested: some states and tribunals treat it as an obligation of due diligence, while others regard it as a policy consideration rather than a binding norm. The ICJ in Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros (1997) took note of the principle without ruling on its customary status. The ITLOS Seabed Mining Advisory Opinion (2011) stated that the precautionary approach is now part of customary international law in the deep seabed context. The principle's precise content — including what level of threat triggers it and what measures are required — remains deeply contested.

state_practice.description: EU environmental law formally incorporates precautionary principle as a binding obligation under Article 191(2) TFEU, shaping regulatory practice across 27 member states
Australia and New Zealand invoked precautionary principle in WTO SPS Agreement disputes and ICJ Nuclear Tests cases to assert obligations on states lacking full scientific certainty

opinio_juris.description: ITLOS Seabed Mining Advisory Opinion (2011) stated that the precautionary approach has been incorporated into the customary international law of the deep seabed area
Rio Declaration Principle 15 (1992), adopted by consensus at UNCED, represents widespread acceptance of the precautionary approach as a guiding norm

title: Common but differentiated responsibilities
summary: Common but differentiated responsibilities (CBDR) holds that all states share responsibility for global environmental problems, but that developed and developing states bear different levels of responsibility reflecting historical contribution and respective capabilities. Rio Declaration Principle 7 and UNFCCC Article 3(1) articulate the principle. CBDR has significant implications for the allocation of mitigation obligations and financial burdens under climate change law. Its status as a binding customary norm is contested: major developed states, particularly the United States, have resisted treating CBDR as a customary rule rather than a political principle, while developing countries and many scholars argue it reflects an established norm of equity. The Paris Agreement (2015) adopts differentiated nationally determined contributions, representing the current operational expression of CBDR in state practice.

state_practice.description: Paris Agreement (2015) adopts differentiated nationally determined contributions (NDCs) reflecting CBDR — with 196 parties it represents the current state of state practice on differentiated climate obligations
G77 plus China consistently invoke CBDR in UNFCCC negotiations to resist equivalent mitigation obligations — persistent developed-country resistance limits its crystallisation as custom

opinio_juris.description: ICJ Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros (1997) — Weeramantry separate opinion treated CBDR as an emerging principle of customary international environmental law grounded in equity

Rio Declaration Principle 7 (1992) adopted by consensus at UNCED reflects widespread acceptance of CBDR as a guiding legal principle — though developed states entered reservations on its customary interpretation

title: Duty to notify and consult on activities with transboundary environmental impact
summary: The duty to notify and consult states potentially affected by activities with significant transboundary environmental impact is a procedural norm of customary international law. It complements the substantive no-harm principle by requiring states to act cooperatively before undertaking potentially harmful activities. The ICJ confirmed the customary basis in Pulp Mills (2010), finding Uruguay failed to notify Argentina adequately before authorising pulp mill construction. ILC Draft Articles on Prevention of Transboundary Harm (2001) codify notification and consultation as Articles 8–9. The duty to negotiate in good faith following notification — though not necessarily to reach agreement — is also part of the customary norm. Disputes concern the timing of notification, what planning stage triggers the obligation, and the weight to be given to the concerns of potentially affected states.
primary_codification: ICJ, Pulp Mills (2010); ILC Draft Articles on Prevention (2001)

state_practice.description: Uruguay failed to adequately notify Argentina before authorising pulp mills on the Uruguay River — ICJ found the customary notification obligation was violated as a matter of international law
Espoo Convention (1991) parties implement notification and consultation procedures for 44 European states — codifying the underlying customary obligation in regional treaty law

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Pulp Mills (2010) confirmed the duty to notify and negotiate in good faith as obligations of customary international law in transboundary environmental contexts
ILC Draft Articles on Prevention of Transboundary Harm (2001), Arts 8–9 — adopted by UNGA as reflecting customary law on notification and consultation

title: Prohibition of the threat or use of force (Article 2(4) as custom)
summary: The prohibition of the threat or use of force against the territorial integrity or political independence of any state, or in any manner inconsistent with the purposes of the United Nations, is enshrined in UN Charter Article 2(4). The ICJ confirmed in the Nicaragua case (1986) that this prohibition exists independently as a norm of customary international law, binding all states including non-UN members. The customary norm is reinforced by the Declaration on Friendly Relations (UNGA Resolution 2625, 1970), adopted by consensus and widely treated as authoritative. The prohibition encompasses direct force and the indirect use of force through armed groups. Exceptions under customary law include self-defence (Article 51) and Security Council authorisation. Contentious questions include the status of humanitarian intervention, pro-democratic intervention, and forcible action against non-state actors operating from another state's territory.

state_practice.description: General state condemnation of Iraq's invasion of Kuwait (1990) as violation of Article 2(4) — UNSC Resolution 660 and 678 adopted treating violation as established customary norm
Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine condemned by 141 states in UNGA ES-11/1 as violation of UN Charter Article 2(4) and customary prohibition on use of force

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) confirmed prohibition on use of force as customary international law independently of the UN Charter, binding on all states
UNGA Resolution 2625 (1970) Declaration on Friendly Relations, adopted by consensus, affirmed prohibition on force as a principle of customary international law

title: Customary right of individual and collective self-defence
summary: The customary right of individual and collective self-defence allows states to use force in response to an armed attack without requiring prior Security Council authorisation. UN Charter Article 51 preserves this inherent right. The ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) confirmed the existence of a customary right running parallel to the treaty right, which may differ in scope. Collective self-defence requires not only that the defending state acts at the request of the victim state, but also that the victim state has declared itself the object of an armed attack. Key disputes involve: the definition of armed attack including the gravity threshold, whether anticipatory self-defence is lawful, whether the right extends to attacks by non-state actors operating from a third state, and the conditions for lawful collective self-defence arrangements such as NATO Article 5.
primary_codification: UN Charter Art. 51; ICJ, Nicaragua (1986); Oil Platforms (2003)

state_practice.description: NATO Article 5 invocation following 9/11 attacks (2001) — the only collective self-defence invocation in NATO history — treated as a valid exercise of collective self-defence under customary international law
United States asserted individual self-defence against IS in Syria and Iraq (2014) citing attacks on US forces — customary law basis for cross-border self-defence against non-state actors

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) confirmed customary right of collective self-defence conditioned on the victim state declaring attack and requesting assistance
ICJ in Oil Platforms (2003) clarified that force used in self-defence must be necessary and proportionate — confirming customary content of the right

title: Principle of non-intervention in internal affairs
summary: The customary principle of non-intervention prohibits states from intervening in matters within the domestic jurisdiction of another state, including by organising, assisting, fomenting, financing, inciting, or tolerating subversive, terrorist, or armed activities directed towards the violent overthrow of another state's regime. The ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) gave the most authoritative statement, holding that the principle prohibits both forcible and coercive interference in another state's internal or external affairs. UNGA Resolution 2625 (1970) codifies the principle as part of customary international law. The principle is distinct from but closely related to the prohibition on use of force — it covers a wider range of coercive acts short of force, including economic coercion. Contested areas include support for pro-democracy movements, sanctions regimes, cyber operations, and the limits of the principle vis-à-vis the Responsibility to Protect.

state_practice.description: Universal condemnation of Soviet interventions in Hungary (1956), Czechoslovakia (1968), and Afghanistan (1979) as violations of non-intervention norm through UNGA resolutions
United States formal legal position asserts non-intervention as a binding customary norm in diplomatic protests against foreign state interference in elections and cyber operations

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) confirmed non-intervention as a rule of customary international law, applicable to both direct force and support for armed groups against another state's government

UNGA Resolution 2625 (1970) — adopted by consensus — authoritatively states that no state has the right to intervene, directly or indirectly, for any reason whatever, in the internal or external affairs of any other state

sources.title: UNGA Resolution ES-4/1 (1956) — Hungary; UNGA Resolution 2131 (1965) — Declaration on Inadmissibility of Intervention

title: Anticipatory self-defence / pre-emptive force
summary: Anticipatory self-defence refers to the use of force before an armed attack has actually occurred, on the basis that it is imminent. The Caroline incident (1837) established the classical formula: self-defence is permissible when the necessity is instant, overwhelming, leaving no choice of means and no moment for deliberation. The legality of anticipatory self-defence under customary international law is actively contested. Some states and scholars argue the customary right of self-defence preserved by UN Charter Article 51 permits action against imminent attacks; others hold that Article 51 requires an actual armed attack to have occurred. The concept of pre-emptive force (against merely potential future threats) goes further still and has even less support. The 2003 Iraq war and Israel's strike on Iraq's Osirak reactor (1981) are the most debated instances. Contemporary debates centre on the right to use force against non-state actors and anticipatory cyber operations.
primary_codification: Caroline formula (1837); UK Attorney General, Iraq (2003); Israeli strikes

state_practice.description: UK Attorney General legal advice on Iraq (2003) relied partly on a broad reading of pre-emptive self-defence under customary international law — contested by most international lawyers

Israel's strike on Iraq's Osirak nuclear reactor (1981) was universally condemned by UNSC Resolution 487 — but Israel asserted anticipatory self-defence, a position supported by US government arguments

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) confirmed that Article 51 requires an armed attack to have occurred but left open the question of anticipatory self-defence under customary law
UNSC Resolution 487 (1981) condemned Israel's strike on Osirak as a clear violation of the UN Charter — the near-universal condemnation undermines a strong custom of anticipatory action

title: Responsibility to Protect (R2P) — intervention pillar
summary: The Responsibility to Protect (R2P) holds that states have a responsibility to protect their populations from genocide, war crimes, ethnic cleansing, and crimes against humanity, and that the international community may act — including through Security Council-authorised force — when a state manifestly fails to do so. The concept was adopted in the 2005 World Summit Outcome (UNGA Resolution 60/1) and applied by the Security Council in Resolution 1973 (Libya, 2011). R2P's status as customary international law is highly contested. The third pillar — coercive action including military intervention — has no clearly established customary basis and depends on Security Council authorisation. States such as Russia, China, India, and Brazil have strongly resisted characterising R2P as customary law. The Libya intervention and its aftermath significantly damaged R2P's prospects as a developing customary norm.
primary_codification: UNGA Res. 60/1 (2005 World Summit Outcome); UNSC Res. 1973 (Libya)

state_practice.description: UNSC Resolution 1973 (2011) authorised all necessary measures to protect civilians in Libya — cited as application of R2P but NATO's implementation was criticised as regime change exceeding the mandate
Russia and China vetoed UNSC resolutions on Syria (2012–2020) citing Libya precedent — persistent objectors to R2P as an emerging customary norm

opinio_juris.description: UNGA Resolution 60/1 (2005 World Summit Outcome) paras 138–139 — adopted by consensus at head-of-state level — reflects broad political acceptance of R2P pillars one and two but not clearly pillar three
Annual UNGA debates on R2P since 2009 show contested state positions — lack of consistent opinio juris on pillar three prevents customary crystallisation

title: Prohibition of aggression as jus cogens
summary: The prohibition of aggression constitutes a peremptory norm of general international law (jus cogens). The crime of aggression was defined for ICC purposes in the Kampala amendments (2010) as the planning, preparation, initiation, or execution of an act of aggression which by its character, gravity, and scale constitutes a manifest violation of the UN Charter. UNGA Resolution 3314 (1974) defines acts of aggression at the state level. The ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) treated the prohibition as a norm of customary law. The ILC's 2022 Conclusions on Jus Cogens include the prohibition of aggression in the illustrative list. Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine has reinvigorated calls for accountability for the crime of aggression and led to proposals for a special tribunal.
primary_codification: UNGA Res. 3314 (1974); ICC Statute Art. 8 bis; ICJ, Nicaragua (1986)

state_practice.description: Nuremberg Tribunal (1946) prosecuted German leaders for crimes against peace — the forerunner of the crime of aggression — establishing individual criminal responsibility for aggressive war as a matter of international law
141 states voted to condemn Russia's invasion of Ukraine as aggression in UNGA ES-11/1 (2022) — near-universal condemnation as violation of the customary prohibition

opinio_juris.description: ILC Conclusions on Jus Cogens (2022) include the prohibition of aggression in the illustrative list of peremptory norms — the most recent authoritative statement of its jus cogens status
ICJ in Nicaragua (1986) confirmed the prohibition on aggression as a customary norm and part of jus cogens, treating it as the most severe form of the prohibition on use of force

title: Sovereign immunity from foreign jurisdiction
summary: Sovereign immunity (or state immunity) shields foreign states from the civil and criminal jurisdiction of domestic courts. It derives from the principles of sovereign equality and non-intervention. Customary international law has evolved from absolute immunity (no domestic court jurisdiction over any foreign state act) toward restrictive immunity, under which states enjoy immunity for governmental acts (acta jure imperii) but not commercial transactions (acta jure gestionis). The ICJ in Jurisdictional Immunities of the State (Germany v Italy, 2012) confirmed that customary international law does not provide an exception to state immunity for serious violations of IHL committed by foreign armed forces. The UN Convention on Jurisdictional Immunities of States and Their Property (2004), though not yet in force, is widely treated as reflecting emerging custom. The central dispute concerns whether jus cogens violations create an exception to immunity.
primary_codification: ICJ, Arrest Warrant (2002); UN Convention on Jurisdictional Immunities (2004)

state_practice.description: UK State Immunity Act 1978, US Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act 1976, and equivalent legislation in over 30 states codify the restrictive theory of immunity as domestic law

Italy's Constitutional Court (Sentenza No 238/2014) refused to give effect to ICJ judgment requiring Italian courts to dismiss suits by WWII victims against Germany, creating ongoing state practice tension

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Jurisdictional Immunities (Germany v Italy, 2012) confirmed customary nature of state immunity for acta jure imperii and rejected jus cogens exception based on survey of state practice and opinio juris
UN Convention on Jurisdictional Immunities of States (2004) — adopted by UNGA consensus — reflects current understanding of customary immunity rules despite not yet being in force

title: Universal jurisdiction over international crimes
summary: Universal jurisdiction permits states to prosecute certain grave international crimes regardless of where they were committed and regardless of the nationality of the accused or the victims. The crimes subject to universal jurisdiction under customary international law include piracy, war crimes, crimes against humanity, genocide, torture, and slave trading. The ICJ in the Arrest Warrant case (2002) noted the debate over universal jurisdiction but declined to resolve it, while individual judges (notably the joint separate opinion of Judges Higgins, Kooijmans, and Buergenthal) addressed its scope. The Princeton Principles (2001) provide an influential scholarly restatement. Controversy persists over whether universal jurisdiction requires the accused's presence in the prosecuting state's territory (conditional universal jurisdiction) or whether it may be exercised in absentia.

state_practice.description: Belgium issued arrest warrant for DRC Foreign Minister Yerodia Ndombasi for crimes against humanity — the case giving rise to ICJ Arrest Warrant judgment on immunity limits
Spain prosecuted Chilean General Pinochet under universal jurisdiction for torture and crimes against humanity committed in Chile — Pinochet arrested in UK in 1998

title: Head of state immunity ratione personae
summary: Head of state immunity ratione personae protects incumbent heads of state, heads of government, and foreign ministers from arrest and criminal proceedings in foreign states while they hold office. The ICJ in the Arrest Warrant case (2002) confirmed that incumbent foreign ministers enjoy full immunity from criminal jurisdiction and inviolability in foreign states as a matter of customary international law. This immunity attaches to the office, not the person or the acts, so it protects even for international crimes allegedly committed before taking office. The tension between this immunity and ICC jurisdiction under Article 27 (which removes immunities for all persons) has generated acute controversy, particularly in relation to the Al Bashir arrest warrant and Security Council referrals. The customary position on immunity in third states not party to the ICC Statute remains disputed.
primary_codification: ICJ, Arrest Warrant (2002); ICC Statute Art. 27 vs Art. 98 tension

state_practice.description: Arrest of Pinochet in UK (1998) at Spain's request led to House of Lords ruling that former (not incumbent) heads of state have no immunity for torture — distinguishing incumbent head of state immunity
States parties to ICC declined to arrest President Al Bashir of Sudan during visits to South Africa (2015), DRC, and other states — significant state practice on the immunity/ICC tension

title: Immunity does not bar accountability for jus cogens violations
summary: The question whether state immunity yields to jus cogens violations — the jus cogens exception to immunity — is one of the most contested areas of contemporary international law. The argument is that since peremptory norms override inconsistent rules, and since state immunity is not jus cogens, serious violations such as torture should strip the offending state of immunity before foreign courts. The ICJ in Jurisdictional Immunities (Germany v Italy, 2012) rejected this argument comprehensively, holding customary international law does not recognise a jus cogens exception to state immunity in civil proceedings. The ECtHR in Al-Adsani v UK (2001) similarly held that Article 6 ECHR does not require a jus cogens exception. Italy's Constitutional Court rejected the ICJ judgment in Sentenza 238/2014 — creating a direct conflict between national constitutional law and international law.
primary_codification: ICJ, Germany v Italy (2012); ECtHR, Al-Adsani v UK (2001)

state_practice.description: Italian courts awarded compensation to victims of Nazi war crimes against Germany — creating the jus cogens exception in domestic law that the ICJ ruled violated customary international law in Germany v Italy (2012)
US courts under the FSIA terrorism exception have provided limited jus cogens-like exceptions to immunity for designated state sponsors of terrorism — a minority state practice

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Jurisdictional Immunities (Germany v Italy, 2012) held by 12 to 3 that customary international law does not currently recognise a jus cogens exception to state immunity — comprehensive rejection of the exception

ECtHR in Al-Adsani v UK (2001) held by 9 to 8 that granting Kuwait immunity from torture claims did not violate Art 6 ECHR — confirming immunity prevails despite jus cogens character of torture prohibition

title: Nationality principle of jurisdiction
summary: The nationality principle of jurisdiction permits a state to prescribe laws governing the conduct of its nationals wherever they are located. It is one of the five classical bases of prescriptive jurisdiction in international law, confirmed by the PCIJ in the Lotus case (1927) and universally accepted in state practice. Most states apply the principle to nationals both as perpetrators (active nationality) and as victims (passive personality). Universal acceptance in domestic criminal codes and treaties establishes the nationality principle as a settled customary rule. Contemporary debates focus on the exercise of nationality jurisdiction over transnational corporations and their activities abroad, and the expansion of nationality jurisdiction in counter-terrorism and anti-corruption contexts such as the US FCPA and UK Bribery Act.
primary_codification: PCIJ, Lotus case (1927); ILC, Extraterritorial Jurisdiction study

state_practice.description: Universal incorporation of nationality jurisdiction in national criminal codes — all 193 UN member states assert jurisdiction over nationals for crimes committed abroad
US FCPA (1977) and UK Bribery Act (2010) assert nationality jurisdiction over corporate nationals for foreign bribery — a major expansion of the nationality principle to corporate actors

opinio_juris.description: PCIJ Lotus case (1927) confirmed territorial and nationality principles are the primary bases of jurisdiction in customary international law, with states having broad discretion
Harvard Research Draft Convention on Jurisdiction with Respect to Crime (1935) and OECD Anti-Bribery Convention treat nationality jurisdiction as an established customary rule

title: Aut dedere aut judicare — extradite or prosecute
summary: Aut dedere aut judicare — the obligation to extradite or prosecute — requires states with custody of a person accused of a serious international crime to either extradite that person to a requesting state or submit the case to their own authorities for prosecution. The ICJ confirmed this obligation under the Convention Against Torture in Belgium v Senegal (2012), its first judgment directly applying aut dedere aut judicare. The customary status beyond the CAT context is debated — the ILC's 2014 Draft Articles were inconclusive. The obligation is triggered by the presence of the accused on state territory and applies regardless of where the offence was committed. Whether it has become a general customary norm applicable to all international crimes beyond specific treaty regimes remains the central open question.
primary_codification: ICJ, Hissène Habré (Belgium v Senegal) (2012); CAT Art. 7

state_practice.description: Belgium prosecuted Hissène Habré (former Chadian president) for torture after Senegal's failure to prosecute — the Extraordinary African Chambers convicted Habré in 2016 following the ICJ judgment

Germany prosecuted Syrian intelligence officer Anwar Raslan (2022) for crimes against humanity under universal jurisdiction after Syria failed to prosecute — an application of aut dedere aut judicare logic

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Belgium v Senegal (2012) confirmed aut dedere aut judicare under the CAT as a treaty and customary obligation — Senegal violated it by failing to prosecute or extradite Habré
Widespread treaty practice — over 50 multilateral treaties include aut dedere aut judicare clauses — reflects opinio juris that serious crimes give rise to this obligation

title: Protective principle of jurisdiction
summary: The protective principle of jurisdiction allows states to prescribe law and assert criminal jurisdiction over acts committed abroad by foreign nationals when those acts threaten the security, integrity, or vital interests of the state. It is classically applied to espionage, treason, counterfeiting of currency, and — more recently — terrorism and cyberattacks. The PCIJ in Lotus (1927) implicitly recognised it, and the Harvard Research Draft on Jurisdiction (1935) codified it as a recognised basis. All major states include the protective principle in their domestic criminal jurisdiction. It is the broadest accepted unilateral jurisdictional claim short of universal jurisdiction. Contemporary debates focus on its application to foreign cyber-attacks on critical national infrastructure and whether expansive US assertions of protective jurisdiction over foreign financial transactions constitute an abuse of process.
primary_codification: PCIJ, Lotus (1927); state practice in national codes

state_practice.description: Universal exercise of protective jurisdiction over counterfeiting of national currency and forged passports — codified in national laws of all major states and subject to bilateral extradition treaties
US assertion of jurisdiction over foreign cyber-attackers targeting critical national infrastructure — applied in indictments of Russian, Chinese, and Iranian nationals under the protective principle

opinio_juris.description: Harvard Research Draft Convention on Jurisdiction with Respect to Crime (1935) codified the protective principle as a universally accepted basis of jurisdiction in customary international law
Restatement (Fourth) of US Foreign Relations Law (2018) § 412 confirms the protective principle as customary international law accepted by all major jurisdictions

title: Principle of sustainable development
summary: The principle of sustainable development holds that economic development must be pursued in a manner that does not compromise the ability of future generations to meet their own needs, integrating environmental protection into the development process. It derives from the Brundtland Commission Report (1987) and is embedded in Rio Declaration Principle 3 and the WTO Agreement preamble. Judge Weeramantry's separate opinion in Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros (1997) characterised sustainable development as a principle of customary international law reconciling the competing requirements of development and environmental protection. Its customary status remains debated: it is frequently invoked in international dispute settlement but courts have not consistently applied it as a binding legal rule rather than an interpretive principle. The concept underpins the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development.
primary_codification: ICJ, Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros (1997) — Weeramantry sep. op.; Rio Declaration

state_practice.description: Universal adoption of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in UNGA Resolution 70/1 (2015) — 193 states — reflects broad state acceptance of sustainable development as a structuring principle of international relations
WTO Appellate Body in Shrimp-Turtle case (1998) invoked sustainable development from the WTO Agreement preamble to interpret trade obligations in light of environmental protection requirements

opinio_juris.description: ICJ Gabčíkovo-Nagymaros, Separate Opinion of Judge Weeramantry (1997) characterised sustainable development as a norm of customary international law representing the balance between development and environmental protection
Rio Declaration Principle 3 (1992) and Agenda 21, adopted by consensus at UNCED, reflect broad opinio juris on sustainable development as an organising legal principle

title: Prohibition of genocide as jus cogens
summary: The prohibition of genocide constitutes a peremptory norm of general international law (jus cogens) and an obligation erga omnes from which no derogation is permitted. The Genocide Convention (1948) defines genocide as acts committed with intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a national, ethnical, racial, or religious group. The ICJ in Bosnia v Serbia (2007) confirmed that the Genocide Convention obligations — including the duty to prevent and punish genocide — reflect customary international law binding all states. The dolus specialis requirement — the specific intent to destroy the group as such — distinguishes genocide from other mass atrocities. Key disputes concern the threshold of group destruction required, the scope of 'in part', the extension to cultural genocide, and the attribution standard for genocide committed by non-state actors.
primary_codification: Genocide Convention Art. II; ICJ, Bosnia v Serbia (2007)

state_practice.description: 190 state parties to the Genocide Convention and domestic genocide legislation in virtually every state reflects universal acceptance of the prohibition as binding customary law
ICTR conviction of Jean-Paul Akayesu (1998) — the first international genocide conviction — applying customary law definition in a non-international armed conflict context

opinio_juris.description: ICJ in Bosnia v Serbia (2007) confirmed the Genocide Convention and its prohibition reflect customary international law with obligations erga omnes to prevent and punish genocide
ILC Conclusions on Jus Cogens (2022) include the prohibition of genocide in the illustrative list of peremptory norms of general international law
